additional_titles.title: Data of an average solar pore

additional_descriptions.description: Dataset containing results of ensemble averaging performed on a set of 13 ("broad" set) and 11 ("narrow" set) solar pores. Videos and FITS files in continuum intensity (Ic), line-of-sight magnetograms (M), vector magnetic intensities (Bp, Bt, Br), radial velocities (V), and perturbations of travel times of helioseismic waves (TT) record the evolution of the average solar pore, with time normalized to hold the emergent phase in the interval [0; 1]. Analyses of the average solar pore, most importantly cross-sections and azimuthal averages, are available as plots and FITS files.

Two sets, a "narrow" set of 11 and a "broad" set of 13, solar pores were selected based on their magnetic classification and manual validation. Observations of these solar pores were downloaded from the SDO/HMI archive. Pore positions were tracked using a circle-fitting algorithm and centre-of-mass in continuum intensity. Based on the tracking data, magnetic flux through each pore was integrated and its evolution in time used to normalize the recordings in time. The growth phase of the pore was fitted using a linear function and the temporal axis of the recording was re-normalized such that the growth phase begins at the start of emergence at normalized time 0 and ends at the maximum flux at normalized time 1. Recordings of individual pores were aligned in both space and time, overlaid and averaged. This was done for each of the following quantites: continuum intensity, line-of-sight magnetogram, vector magnetic intensity, radial velocity, and perturbations of helioseismic travel times (in out-in, east-west, and south-north geometries and the f and p1 through p4 modes). The results were then analyzed using a small set of simple tools, most importantly the evolution of pore cross-section and azimuthal average in time.

A TAR archive for each physical quantity and each set contains a sequence of (2D) images in FITS format (https://fits.gsfc.nasa.gov/fits_home.html) with the results of ensemble averaging. Each FITS file contains the averaged image, the Poisson noise and the standard deviations. Coordinate systems and physical units are described in the CRPIXn, CDELTn, and BUNIT headers. Each FITS file also contains the TIMENORM header containing the normalized time of the image.

metadata.title: WRF simulations on impact of urban areas on cloud cover and precipitation over cities in Central Europe

additional_descriptions.description: It is well known that urban areas have an effect on relevant meteorological variables such as temperature and wind speed, but the effect on cloud cover and precipitation is less study. To assess this impact, we use the WRF model over a domain covering Central Europe. Here we present the results obtained from a 15-members ensemble to quantify the impact of urban areas on temperature at 2 m (T2), specific humidity (Q2), cloud cover (CLDFRA_comp) and hourly precipitation (hourly_prec) over Central Europe from 2008 to 2017. These data are the basis for the analysis presented in the paper "Long-term impact of urban areas on meteorological conditions over Central Europe" by Villalba-Pradas, A., Karlický, J., Huszár, P., Žak, M., and Halenka, T.,  Annals of the New York Academy of Science, 2025  (check for final published version).

Data are based WRF simulations over a a 9 km x 9 km horizontal resolution domain (190 x 166 gridpoints) centered in Prague and covering Central Europe. The simulated period covers 10 years, from 2008 to 2017. These simulations were driven by ERA5 using CORINE Land cover data (CLC 2012 version).

A total of 15 simulations were conducted with different combinations of the urban, convection and microphysics parameterizations: a) NU_G3D – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. b) NU_GF – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. c) NU_NT – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. d) NU_P3 – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. e) NU_WDM6 – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. f) BULK_G3D – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. g) BULK_GF – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. h) BULK_NT – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. i) BULK_P3 – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. j) BULK_WDM6 – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. k) SLUCM_G3D – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. l) SLUCM_GF – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. m) SLUCM_NT – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. n) SLUCM_P3 – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. o) SLUCM_WDM6 – simulation with nthe SLUCM urban scheme, the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. Data are provided for the following variables: T2, Q2, cloud fraction and hourly precipitation. Each file contains one ensemble member and one variable at hourly resolution. Format of data: netCDF.

metadata.title: Raw data for A1901 and A2103
metadata.publisher: Ústav fotoniky a elektroniky AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Molecular dynamics simulations trajectories of kinesin motor domain on microtubule in an intense electric field. Four different electric field directions, and four different field strengths were explored. The data can serve further to researchers in the field of bionanotechnology, bioelectromagnetics and biophysics.
Gromacs 2018 software and CHARMM36(mar2019) force field was used to generate molecular dynamics trajectories.

metadata.title: Raw data for A1805 and A2006 papers
metadata.publisher: Ústav fotoniky a elektroniky AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Molecular dynamics simulations trajectories of microtubule in an intense electric field. Four electric field directions, two different field strengths, two different temperatures, and three different ion concentrations were explored. The data can serve further to researchers in the field of bionanotechnology, bioelectromagnetics and biophysics.

CUDA accelerated version of AMBER software with ff14SB force field in combination with the TIP3P water model for solvation was used to generate molecular dynamics trajectories. See more details in the linked full paper.

metadata.title: Raw nanopore long-read sequencing data for 61 Klebsiella pneumoniae isolates – PromethION run 240216-FNB-P2S

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains raw nanopore long-read sequencing data generated using the Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) PromethION platform (run 240216-FNB-P2S). It includes 61 clinical isolates of Klebsiella pneumoniae obtained from the University Hospital Brno (Brno, Czechia). The data are provided in pod5 format exactly as exported from the instrument and include both pass and fail reads.

A total of 61 Klebsiella pneumoniae isolates were collected between April 2018 and November 2023 from multiple departments of the University Hospital Brno (Brno, Czechia). Genomic DNA was extracted using the DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit (Qiagen). DNA purity and concentration were assessed using a NanoDrop spectrophotometer and a Qubit 3.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Sequencing libraries were prepared using the Rapid Barcoding Kit 96 V14 (Oxford Nanopore Technologies). All samples were sequenced on an Oxford Nanopore Technologies PromethION 2 Solo instrument using R10.4.1 flow cells as part of run 240216-FNB-P2S. Data were generated natively in POD5 format and exported directly from the run directory. During acquisition, the instrument performed real-time basecalling and automatically classified reads into pass (reads with a minimum quality score of 10) and fail categories. Both pass and fail POD5 files are included in this dataset.

Sequencing platform: Oxford Nanopore Technologies PromethION 2 Solo. Flow cell: R10.4.1. Acquisition software: MinKNOW v23.11.5. Data format: POD5. Dataset structure: each isolate contains 2 directories: pod5_pass (reads with a minimum quality score of 10) and pod5_fail (reads with quality scores below 10).

metadata.title: 3DZD: Protein structural embeddings of ESM Atlas

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v4 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

The embedding method was first published in the paper Real-Time Structure Search and Structure Classification for AlphaFold Protein Models. The embedding uses 3D Zernike descriptor (3DZD), an emerging technique to describe molecular surfaces. The 3DZD is a series expansion of a mathematical three-dimensional function, and thus, a tertiary structure is represented compactly by a vector of coefficients of terms in the series. 3DZD is invariant to the rotation of the represented target object. The embedding implements 3D Zernike moments as a vector of length 120, which is also the length of this embedding.

The archive contains protein embeddings for more than 700-million proteins from ESM Atlas. Protein data are divided into 700 batches of approximately 1M proteins in each. All data are stored in a parquet format and can be opened as pandas DataFrame. The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. Note: There are approximately 250k proteins missing from the ESM Atlas. This is a known issue (https://github.com/facebookresearch/esm/issues/405). The list of missing proteins is in missing_proteins.txt. For more info and to get in touch see https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark. The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: Red mark syndrom disease video data

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset consists of the video records of the rainbow trout in three cultivation units infected by Red mark syndrome disease (RMS). The videos were recorded by the digital camera placed in the cultivation tank's waterproof box. Each video is 30 minutes long. The records were taken during 3 months. Two tanks, TANK_01 and TANK_03 contain infected fish. TANK_20 contains control non-infected fish. The dataset documents the progress of the RMS visible signs on the fish skin from early to late stages. The dataset was used to develop a remote early warning system for automatic disease detection in aquaculture production. It can be used for the studies in the filed of automation of aquaculture: fish individual identification, disease analysis, fish behavior analysis, or fish detection.

Experimental animals

The rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) used in the study were hatched at DTU and raised under pathogen-free conditions. At a size of around 50 g, they were individually tagged with a passive integrated transponder (PIT) tag in the dorsal musculature under anaesthesia. Before experimentation, they were moved to the infection unit at DTU. The infection was caused by cohabitation with RMS-affected seeder rainbow trout. The size of the 29 seeders used for the experiment was 203±26 g, 25±1.2 cm. A total of 180 healthy rainbow trout were used (160±38 g,  22.9±1.7 cm at the start of the experiment and 348±85 g, 29±1.8 cm at the end on day 83). One hundred twenty were infected by cohabitation, and 60 were non-infected controls. The seeders were produced 61 days before the cohabitation with the experimental fish. The cohabitation of the 29 seeders and 120 cohabitants was from 20.9.2023 to 4.10.2023 when the seeders were removed. The 120 cohabitants were subsequently divided into two experimental tanks (tank TANK_01 and TANK_03). A third similar tank was used for 60 uninfected fish as a control group (tank TANK_20). See the detailed schedule in Figure 1. [PC1] All fish were kept in flow-through 1m3 tanks supplied with a flow of fresh water at a rate of ~130 L/h. Water temperature was 12.0+/-0.5 °C, the light regime was 12:12 h light: dark, and the fish were fed commercial 3mm pellets at a rate of 1% total body weight daily using clock feeders. One fish per tank was humanely euthanised at two-week intervals, and skin lesions were sampled for histological confirmation of RMS. The experiment was terminated on 12.12.2023. The experiment was conducted under the animal experimentation license 2019-15-0201-00159-C2 issued by the Danish Animal Experiments Inspectorate. The fish was sacrificed at the end of the experiment.

 

Figure 1. Experimental schedule.

RMS symptoms

RMS is a skin disease, and the primary clinical manifestation is characteristic changes in the mucosal surface of the fish. The initial symptoms are often subtle and may appear as lighter patches of skin. These changes usually have a dorsoventrally elongated shape if found on the flanks. They gradually develop a pinkish hue and turn bright red in later stages (see Figure 2). Lesions are typically from 2 to 10 cm in diameter. Apart from the colour change (due to hyperemia/haemorrhaging), hallmark pathological signs of RMS are oedema (raised skin) and scale loss (due to resorption) at more advanced stages of lesion development. Ulcers can develop in severe cases, often as a result of secondary bacterial infections. This complicates the course of the disease and potentially results in fish mortality if therapeutic measures are not initiated in time (Galeotti et al., 2021), but it also jeopardises a correct diagnosis. However, in the semi-controlled model at DTU, ulceration is rare.

 

Figure 2. Examples of RMS visual symptoms. Left – early symptom (6 weeks after cohabitation [PC2] ), light spot on the body, Middle – the pinkish spot (8 weeks after cohabitation), Right – late symptom, dark red large lesion with missing scales (10 weeks after cohabitation).

Fish video records

The video records simulated the real fish cultivation conditions similar to those in fish production. Videos were recorded at regular intervals (every few days, 2-6 times per week) using an RGB digital camera (See3CAM_CU27) submerged in the housing in the fish tank, ensuring consistent monitoring of symptom progression throughout the experiment, see Figure 1. The HD resolution of 1280  × 720 pixels and a frame rate of 80 fps were used for video recording. The camera in housing was placed on a plastic tube holder; see Figure 3. The camera was placed in a waterproof transparent plastic box connected by an optical USB cable to the recording computer. The secure holder enables removing the camera from the tank during tank maintenance. The holder was equipped with a water-resistant LED light (20W, 5500K). Lighting was used to improve visibility in the tank, maximising overall image quality and increasing camera framerate. Both the light and the camera were remotely controlled from the computer. The light was switched on only during the recording period.

 

 

Figure 3. Left – The camera system consists of a plastic holder, an LED light, and the camera enclosed in a waterproof transparent box. Right – the top view of the tank with the fish.

All three tanks (two with infected fish and one control tank) were equipped with the camera system. The video recording started on October 9, five days after the seeders were removed. The earlier period was not recorded because no RMS symptoms were expected (based on the knowledge of RMS development). The thirty-minute video records were taken during the whole experiment for all tanks. Four video records were taken for the control tank, and forty video records were taken for two tanks with infected fish. The last video records were captured on December 10, two days before the last evaluation and experiment termination. In total, 44 video records (22 hours) of fish swimming were recorded. The fish freely moved in front of the camera. The cameras were focused at a distance greater than 15 cm to optimise the field of view and maintain focus on the majority of the fish swimming in the tank, Figure 4.

Figure 4. Examples of underwater video records from the camera system. The two images demonstrate the difference in the illumination conditions.

Data preprocessing and annotation

Video recordings were preprocessed and manually annotated to prepare data for training the CNN-based detector. Providing ground-truth data enabled reliable localisation and identification of the progress of visual signs on the fish skin at different stages. This process generated Video Image Dataset (VID).

The VID images dataset was extracted from videos to create a dataset for model training from video records. All video records were manually checked in the VirtualDub software, and the frame with visible signs on the fish was determined. When the sign was visible, the short track was used to extract every 10th frame. These frames represent the fish with visible signs at different positions, orientations, and rotations and with light reflections on the fish's body. These parameters change during the fish's swimming, and the training dataset should consist of the images under the different conditions to generalise well. The same five classes used for still image annotation were used for video image annotation. The total number of annotated images was 1991. The fish bodies were also annotated for the VID dataset to enable a more detailed analysis of the video record. The whole fish body rectangle, fish eye rectangle and dorsal fin rectangle were annotated for the fish with annotated signs of early or late RMS, see Figure 5. This triplet was annotated only if all three parts were visible. In total, 1500 fish were annotated. The number of early RMS was 1426, and the number of late RMS was 688.

Figure 5. Annotated images from short video sequence of early RMS symptoms on one fish. The early RMS (green), fish (pink), eye (red), dorsal fin (orange) and snout/lower jaw (dark green) was annotated on all images.

The dataset is stored in four ZIP files.

TANK_01.zip

TANK_03.zip

TANK_20.zip

Images from videos.zip

Images and annotations.zip

 

 

TANK_01.zip - contains 17 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231129-190001.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231130-220508.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231205-185541.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 55 minutes long

        - The file 20231206-194522.mp4 need not to be read properly



 

TANK_03.zip - contains 23 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231127-161610.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231204-192209.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 47 minutes long

        - The file 20231107-174838.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 46 minutes long

 

 

TANK_20.zip - contains 4 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231024-171335.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 59 minutes long

 

Images from videos.zip – contains three subdirectories: TANK_01, TANK_03, andTANK_20. Each subdirectory contains subdirectories with the images selected from the particular video file of the tank. The name of subdirectory corresponds to the name of video file the images were selected from. The sub sub directory contain the images in png image format. The description of image selection is provided in Metodologie part of the dataset description.

 

Images and annotations.zip – it contains two folders:

-          RMS only

-          RMS+fish

 

-          Each folder contains images subfolder, labels subfolder and classes.txt file. These files contain the all images selected form the video files and manually annotated by 6 or 9 classes. The format of annotation is YOLO bounding boxes. The description of image annotation is provided in Metodologie part of the dataset description.

metadata.title: Data for "Ozonization for MALDI-2 MS imaging of carbon–carbon double bond positional isomers of phosphatidylethanolamines in biological tissues"

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains MALDI(-2) MS and MS/MS spectra and images of tissues treated with ozone recorded on timsTOF fleX MALDI-2 instrument (Bruker Daltonics GmbH) in the original file format distributed into folders named according to the figures in the linked publication. Exceptions are Figure S6 containing ESI MS/MS spectra from Q-Exactive (Thermo Fisher Scientific) in .raw format and Figure S10 contains data converted to .sbd file format (SCiLS base data) used by SCiLS Lab software (Bruker Daltonics GmbH) to reduce data size. Figure 1 contains MALDI-2 MS and MALDI-2 MS2 of PE 34:1 ozonide recorded from mouse brain. Figure 2 contains MALDI-2 MS2I of PE 36:1 isomers in the low-grade adenocarcinoma tissue. Figure 3 two MALDI-2 MS2I of PE 34:1 and PE 36:1 isomers in prenatal embryo, Figure 4 MALDI-2 MS2I of PC 34:1 isomers in mouse pup. The remaining data covers the MALDI MS and MSI experiments is provided in the Supporting Information.

Sections of biological tissues (6 -20 μm thick) were prepared using a cryomicrotome  at −20 °C and thaw-mounted on standard non-conductive microscope glass slides. Tissues were desalted by immersion into a cold (6 °C) 50 mmol L−1 aqueous solution of ammonium acetate three times for 5 s. The ozonization was carried out in a 500 mL glass flask with the glass slide positioned at the bottom. The ozone-enriched gas supplied by an ozone generator at a flow rate of 0.130 L min−1 was introduced to initiate the reaction. Ozone production was ∼2.76 g per minute (calculated based on the manufacturer's information). Afterwards, the DHB matrix was applied to the tissue sections by sublimation using a home-built apparatus with an evacuated chamber. In brief, 1.5 mL of a 20 mg mL−1 DHB solution in acetone was loaded into a preheated brass reservoir, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate. The sublimation proceeded for 15 min at ∼5 × 10−3 mbar and the matrix reservoir temperature of 130 °C. The matrix deposition resulted in a coverage of 330 ± 20 μg cm−2, calculated by weighing the glass slide before and after sublimation. Most of the MS and MSI measurements were conducted using a prototype timsTOF fleX MALDI-2 mass spectrometer (Bruker Daltonics) in the positive ion mode with the activated high sensitivity detection and the ion detection range set to m/z 300–1000 and m/z 400–800 for full-scan MALDI(-2) MS and MS2 measurements, respectively. The mass resolving power of the instrument was 40,000 (FWHM) in the phospholipid mass range, and mass accuracy was ≤2 ppm with external calibration. In MALDI-2 MS2 experiments, CID with nitrogen as the collision gas was employed. The optimal collision energy (CE) was set to 20 eV, the collision cell RF voltage to 2000 V. The MSI data were evaluated and visualized using SCiLS Lab MVS (vs 2023a, Bruker Daltonics). The ESI MS data were recorded on a Q-Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) with a mass resolving power of 140,000.

metadata.title: CAMx simulations on BVOC impact over central Europe

additional_descriptions.description:
Biogenic Volatile Organic Compounds (BVOC) emissions play an important role in atmospheric chemistry and ozone pollution. To assess their impact on air-pollution, regional chemistry transport models have to be applied that account for the transport, chemical transformation and deposition of pollutants. Here we present the results of CAMx simulations to quantify the impact of BVOC emissions on ozone (O3), formaldehyde (HCHO), hydroxyl radical (OH) and peroxy radicals near surface concentrations for central Europe over a decade-long period from 2007 to 2016. 

These data are the basis for the analysis presented by:
Liaskoni, M., Huszár, P., Bartík, L., Prieto Perez, A. P., Karlický, J., and Šindelářová, K.: The long-term impact of BVOC emissions on urban ozone patterns over central Europe: contributions from urban and rural vegetation, EGUsphere [preprint], https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2024-2027, 2024.  (check for final published version)

Data are based CAMx simulations over the period of 2007-2016 over a 9 km x 9 km horizontal resolution regional central European domain (189x165 gridpoints). These simulations were driven by WRF decadal simulations and anthropogenic emissions were taken from the MACC-III dataset. BVOC emissions were calculated based on WRF meteorology with LAI and PFT data extracted from MODIS satellite observations.

Multiple simulations were carried out to calculate the impact of: 1) all BVOC emissions 2) impact of only the urban BVOC emissions.
a) allBVOC - simulation with all BVOC emissions
b) noBVOC - simulation with no BVOC emissions
c) nuBVOC - simulation with only nourban BVOC emissions
d) uBVOC - simulation with only urban BVOC emissions
e)2nuBVOC - sensitivity simulation with double urban BVOC emissions
f)0.5nuBVOC - sensitivity simulation with half urban BVOC emissions
Data are provided for the following chemical species: O3, NO2, HCHO, OH, HO2 and RO2

Each file stands for one simulation and one species and contain hourly near surface data. Format of data: netcdf.

metadata.title: 3D-af-Surfer: Protein structural embeddings of AlphaFold DB

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v3 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

The embedding method was first published in the paper Real-Time Structure Search and Structure Classification for AlphaFold Protein Models. The embedding uses 3D Zernike descriptor (3DZD), an emerging technique to describe molecular surfaces. The 3DZD is a series expansion of a mathematical three-dimensional function, and thus, a tertiary structure is represented compactly by a vector of coefficients of terms in the series. 3DZD is invariant to the rotation of the represented target object. The embedding implements 3D Zernike moments as a vector of length 120, which is also the length of this embedding.

The archive contains protein embeddings for 214M million proteins from AFDB v3. Protein data are divided into 25,339 batches of approximately 10,000 proteins in each. All data are stored in pickle format and can be opened as pandas Data Frame. To find a specific protein, use batch-to-protein-index.csv, which lists the contents of each batch.


The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. We benchmarked this embedding, assessing the correlation between protein structural information and embedding similarity. The metric used to assess structural similarity was a structure alignment method TM-Align; the embedding distance was computed as Euclidean (or Cosine) distance between the vectors. These results are available at https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark . The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.
